title: Soft colourful light: map input to colour
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire three knobs to three input pins and the RGB LED to three PWM pins, upload the Arduino sketch, and turn the knobs to mix any colour by hand. Under the colours sits the real lesson — map(), the single line that rescales one range onto another, so a knob reading of 0 to 4095 can drive a colour channel of 0 to 255.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for soft colourful light: three rotary potentiometers with their middle pins on GPIO 12, 13, and 14, and an RGB LED whose red, green, and blue legs run through 220 ohm resistors to GPIO 38, 39, and 40, with the long common pin joined to 3.3 volts, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: 3 × rotary potentiometers
parts.explanation: A potentiometer is a resistor with a third pin on a movable contact. Turning the shaft slides that contact along the resistor, so the voltage on the middle pin sweeps smoothly between the two ends. Wire the ends to 3.3V and ground and the knob becomes a voltage dial your ADC pin can read.

parts.name: RGB LED
parts.explanation: An RGB LED is three tiny LEDs — red, green, and blue — in one dome. Set each one's brightness with PWM and your eye blends them into a single colour. All off is dark; all full is white; everything else is a mix — the same trick a screen uses.

parts.name: 3 × 220 Ω resistors
parts.explanation: A resistor is a small component whose whole job is to limit how much current flows through a wire. Current is the flow of electricity through the circuit; a resistor throttles that flow down to a safe amount. Its strength is measured in ohms (Ω) — a larger number resists harder and lets less current through. A LED wired straight to power pulls far more current than it can survive and burns out at once, so it always sits in series with a resistor. The 220 Ω value here holds the current gentle enough to light the LED and keep it alive. The coloured bands print the resistor's value, and it works the same either way round — a resistor has no positive or negative end.

parts.name: Jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: The board reads this knob to set how much red shows.
The board reads this knob to set how much green shows.
The board reads this knob to set how much blue shows.
The two ends give the middle pin a full sweep from zero to full voltage.
PWM on this pin sets the red brightness.
PWM on this pin sets the green brightness.
PWM on this pin sets the blue brightness.
All three colours share this positive pin so a colour lights when its pin goes LOW.

coachInstructions: Land the core idea early — map(value, inLow, inHigh, outLow, outHigh) drops a reading at the same fraction of the output range as it sits in the input range, and here it rescales a 0 to 4095 knob onto a 0 to 255 colour channel.
Show the composition — Day 13's analogRead gives the number, Day 9's RGB channel takes the brightness, and map() is the adapter between them.
Anchor the pairing early — knob on GPIO 12 drives red on 38, 13 drives green on 39, 14 drives blue on 40 — and have the learner say it back before debugging.
If one colour never changes, check that knob's middle wire on 12/13/14 and its outer legs on 3.3V and GND before touching code.
If turning a knob moves the wrong colour, the RGB legs are swapped on 38/39/40 — fix the wiring order rather than the sketch.
The 256-minus write is Day 9's common-anode inversion and stands apart from the mapping — explain it on request and keep the sketch's map input top of 4096 exactly as written.
For the challenge, swapping map's output ends reverses a channel and narrowing its input top clips the top of the knob's travel — both are one-line edits that show what the four map() numbers do.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython pairs the knobs to colours in reverse order (12 drives 40) and that difference is expected, so never "fix" it.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Place the three potentiometers on the breadboard with room to turn each knob.
Wire each knob's outer legs to 3.3V and GND so its middle pin can sweep the full range.
Run the middle pins to GPIO 12, 13, and 14 — red, green, and blue in that order.
Wire the RGB LED's red leg through a 220 Ω resistor to GPIO 38, green to GPIO 39, blue to GPIO 40.
Run the LED's long common pin to 3.3V.
Compare every wire to the chart before you plug in USB.
Open Sketch_10.2_SoftColorfulLight.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Turn each knob in turn and watch its colour rise and fall in the dome.

arduino.explanation: Three matched arrays pair each knob with a colour leg — position 0 links the knob on 12 to the red leg on 38 and so on down the line.
Reads one knob and rescales it proportionally — where the reading sits between 0 and 4096 is where the colour value lands between 0 and 255.
Day 9's common-anode inversion — this dome lights when its pin goes LOW so the sketch subtracts the value before writing it.
Gives each colour leg its own hardware PWM channel at 1 kHz with 8-bit brightness.

micropython.explanation: The whole chain in one line — read the knob, rescale to the 10-bit duty range, and subtract for the common-anode dome.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: map() finds where a number sits in its input range and drops it at the same spot in the output range — plain proportional arithmetic.

notes.title: Match the input ends to the real reading
notes.body: The input ends should be the smallest and largest numbers the sensor truly reaches. Claim a top of 4096 when the knob only reaches 3600 and the colour never quite hits full. Claim a top of 2048 when the knob reaches 4095 and everything past halfway pins at full and stops changing. The output uses its whole range only when the input ends are honest about the reading.

notes.title: map() rescales, it does not clamp
notes.body: map() is straight proportional arithmetic, so a reading outside the stated input range lands outside the output range too. When a value might stray past its ends, fence it with constrain() first — the pairing you met in Day 15's glossary.

notes.title: Where 4096 and 255 come from
notes.body: 4096 is the top of the ADC's 12-bit scale and 255 is the top of an 8-bit PWM channel, so this map squeezes a 4096-step reading down onto a 256-step brightness. Change the chip or the settings and those numbers move, and map() still only needs the four ends.

expectedOutputExample: Turn one knob and one colour sweeps smoothly from dark to full while the other two hold steady.
Set the knobs in combinations and the dome mixes the shades — red and green up makes yellow.
All three knobs up glows white; all three down leaves the dome dark.
test.successCriteria: Every knob turned down means darkness by design — white takes all three channels at full.

firstChecks: Check that knob's middle wire on GPIO 12, 13, or 14 — the board is reading air.

troubleshooting.symptom: Turning the red knob changes the wrong colour
firstChecks: The RGB legs are in the wrong order — red goes to GPIO 38, green to 39, blue to 40.

troubleshooting.symptom: The dome stays dark with knobs up
firstChecks: Check the common pin — it goes to 3.3V.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Swap red's output ends to run the colour backwards, then narrow red's input top and watch the top of the knob's travel clip at full.
